content:
The Technology That Finally Ended “It Works on My Machine”

5 min read
Just now

--

Disclaimer: This article uses simplified examples and fictional scenarios to explain Docker in the easiest way possible. The stories are not real and are included only to help readers understand the concepts better.
The first issue I investigated as a Linux Administrator was not caused by bad code.
A server crash did not cause it.
A network outage did not cause it.
The application worked perfectly on the developer’s laptop.
The application failed immediately after deployment.
After hours of troubleshooting, the reason turned out to be surprisingly simple.
The developer was using a different software version than the production server.
A small difference in the environment was enough to break the application.
At the time, I thought this was a rare incident.
It wasn’t.
The more systems I worked on, the more I realized the same problem appeared everywhere.
Developers spent countless hours debugging applications that were perfectly fine. The real problem was that the environment where the application was running was different.
